title: Intel Accelerates Next-Generation Foundry 'Race Race' with 'Hi-NA' EUV Deployment... Samsung Electronics Remains Cautious
gist: Samsung is deliberately delaying broad High-NA EUV deployment to protect foundry profitability even though it already owns two ASML High-NA systems, while Intel has become the first chipmaker to use High-NA in volume production (18A Panther Lake) and TSMC pursues its own roadmap-driven pace (A16 slipping to 2027, new A12/A13/N2U nodes through 2029) — illustrating that the three leading foundries are taking distinct paths to leading-edge manufacturing.

insights.claim: Samsung Electronics is delaying broad deployment of High-NA EUV lithography despite already having installed two of ASML's latest High-NA systems, prioritizing deployment timing based on economics over being first to use the newest equipment.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Samsung foundry customers and investors should not expect a near-term High-NA-driven capacity ramp; capex discipline is being prioritized over technology leadership optics.
insights.quote: advanced manufacturing leadership is no longer defined solely by adopting the newest equipment, but by deploying it when the economics make sense.

insights.claim: According to industry reports, Samsung believes the depreciation and operating costs of High-NA tools outweigh the immediate manufacturing benefits while its foundry utilization remains below optimal levels, so its installed High-NA systems will mainly be used for R&D, process optimization, and pilot production rather than large-scale manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Signals Samsung's foundry business is still capacity-underutilized; watch Samsung foundry utilization rates as the leading indicator for when High-NA capex re-accelerates.
insights.quote: the company believes the substantial depreciation and operating costs associated with High-NA tools outweigh the immediate manufacturing benefits, particularly while foundry utilization remains below optimal levels.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Samsung, however, appears more focused...'

insights.claim: If foundry demand strengthens and production economics improve, Samsung is expected to be well positioned to activate its already-installed High-NA capacity more aggressively, meaning its current delay is a hedge rather than a permanent retreat from advanced manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Samsung's dormant High-NA capacity is latent optionality; a demand inflection (e.g., AI-driven foundry orders) could be a fast-activation catalyst worth monitoring.
insights.quote: If foundry demand strengthens and production economics improve, the company is expected to be well positioned to activate its installed High-NA capacity more aggressively.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'For Samsung, delaying mass production...'

insights.claim: ASML has confirmed its High-NA EUV lithography platform has entered high-volume logic manufacturing, with Intel becoming the first semiconductor manufacturer to use the technology in volume production for selected layers of its 18A Panther Lake processors.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Intel has secured a genuine, verifiable manufacturing-readiness lead in next-gen EUV tooling ahead of TSMC and Samsung, a rare tangible proof point for its foundry turnaround.
insights.quote: Intel becoming the first semiconductor manufacturer to use the technology in volume production for selected layers of its 18A Panther Lake processors.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'The decision comes at a time...'

insights.claim: TSMC has extended its process technology roadmap through 2029, introducing future nodes A12, A13, and N2U, while pushing the timing of A16 production back to 2027, choosing to balance node scaling with manufacturing efficiency rather than rush High-NA adoption.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: TSMC's more gradual High-NA adoption versus Intel implies lower near-term capex urgency risk; the A16 slip to 2027 is a scheduling signal for when leading-edge capacity actually comes online.
insights.quote: TSMC has extended its process technology roadmap through 2029, introducing future nodes including A12, A13, and N2U while adjusting the timing of A16 production to 2027.

themes.theme: Divergent foundry strategies and High-NA economics

insights.claim: Each new semiconductor process generation demands significantly higher capital investment, and High-NA EUV represents one of the industry's largest single equipment expenditures, so chipmakers must justify it through sufficient production volume and customer demand despite its higher resolution and reduced multi-patterning complexity.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: High-NA capex intensity is a structural gate on foundry economics; foundries with weaker near-term demand visibility face real pressure to delay adoption regardless of technical benefits.
insights.quote: High-NA EUV represents one of the industry's largest equipment expenditures.
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'The cautious strategy also reflects...'

insights.claim: The divergence between Intel's early production deployment, Samsung's cost-focused strategy, and TSMC's roadmap-driven approach shows there is no single path to semiconductor manufacturing leadership; success depends on integrating the most advanced lithography into a sustainable manufacturing and business strategy, not merely owning it first.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Investors should judge foundry competitiveness by capex discipline and ROI timing rather than by who adopts new equipment or nodes first.
insights.quote: there is no single path to semiconductor leadership. Success will depend not only on owning the most advanced lithography systems, but also on integrating them into a sustainable manufacturing and business strategy.

insights.claim: AI processors, high-performance computing devices, and next-generation mobile chips are expected to keep driving demand for leading-edge silicon, making High-NA EUV an increasingly important manufacturing technology over time.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Secular AI/HPC demand is the driver that could eventually force even cautious players like Samsung into broader High-NA deployment; watch AI-driven order volumes as the trigger.
insights.quote: As AI processors, high-performance computing devices, and next-generation mobile chips continue driving demand for leading-edge silicon, High-NA EUV is widely expected to become an increasingly important manufacturing technology.
